message:
fix(ft-api): tensor_unique uses IEEE == instead of epsilon merge

tensor_unique compared values via `(u - v).abs() < f64::EPSILON ||
(u.is_nan() && v.is_nan())`, which silently:
  - merged any pair of values whose absolute distance fell below
    machine epsilon (≈ 2.22e-16) — including obviously distinct
    subnormals like 5e-324 and 1e-323 (these differ by 5e-324, far
    below f64::EPSILON), and
  - merged every NaN bit pattern into a single output entry.

Both behaviours diverge from PyTorch's torch.unique and NumPy's
numpy.unique, which use IEEE 754 `==`:
  - 0.0 == -0.0 is true → merged (matches PyTorch)
  - NaN == NaN is false → every NaN is its own unique entry
  - Distinct subnormals compare != and stay distinct

Replace the heuristic with raw `u == v` so all three contracts hold
automatically.

Tests:
  unique_keeps_distinct_subnormals: input
    [5e-324, 1e-323, 5e-324, 0.0] now produces sorted uniques
    [0.0, 5e-324, 1e-323] with counts [1, 2, 1]. The pre-fix
    epsilon-merge would have collapsed all three subnormals into a
    single entry.
  unique_treats_zero_and_negzero_as_equal: -0.0 and 0.0 merge into
    one unique (PyTorch behaviour).
  unique_keeps_each_nan_distinct: input
    [NaN, 1.0, NaN, 2.0, NaN] keeps all 3 NaNs as separate uniques.

All 10 existing unique-family tests continue to pass — bit-for-bit
forward parity preserved on the integer-valued cases that don't hit
the epsilon edge.

Workspace: 1119 ft-api tests green (1116 + 3 new), clippy clean.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
